description:
A postsecondary psychology teacher has collected facial emotion recognition data from participants using an Ekman-style task. The data is located at ~/pebl/data/emotion_recognition_data.csv with columns: participant_id, trial_num, true_emotion, response_emotion, and rt_ms. 

Calculate individual emotion-specific accuracies, apply clinical exclusion criteria to identify participants meeting the rules, and compute a normalized group confusion matrix for the valid participants. 

Exclusion Rules:
1. 'Random Responder': Exclude any participant whose overall accuracy across all trials is strictly less than 25% (< 0.25).
2. 'Amygdala Deficit': Exclude any participant whose accuracy specifically on 'Fear' trials is strictly less than 10% (< 0.10) AND whose combined accuracy on all OTHER emotions (non-Fear trials) is strictly greater than 70% (> 0.70).

Save the results as a JSON file to ~/pebl/analysis/emotion_report.json. The JSON must contain a 'participants' list. Each participant entry must include 'id', 'overall_accuracy', and 'emotion_accuracy' (a dictionary mapping each emotion to its accuracy as a decimal). If a participant meets either exclusion rule, their entry should instead contain 'id', 'excluded': true, and a 'reason' string. The JSON must also contain a 'group_confusion_matrix' object computed ONLY from the valid (non-excluded) participants. The matrix should be a dictionary of dictionaries where matrix[true_emotion][response_emotion] gives the probability of that response, normalized such that the probabilities for each true_emotion row sum to exactly 1.0.
